attributes.stem: A 45-year-old man is admitted to hospital following emergency laparoscopic appendicectomy for perforated appendicitis. He has a medical history of severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) requiring two hospital admissions in the past year, and previous myocardial infarction with coronary artery stenting. His medications include tiotropium, salbutamol, aspirin, metoprolol, and atorvastatin. He had eaten a large meal two hours before the onset of symptoms. The surgery was performed under general anaesthetic without complications. In the post-anaesthetic care unit, he develops acute shortness of breath and a productive cough with frothy sputum. His pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 28/min, SpO2 88% on room air, and temperature 38.2°C. On examination, he has bilateral coarse crackles on chest auscultation. A chest X-ray shows new bilateral infiltrates.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features typical of aspiration pneumonitis, which occurs when gastric contents are inhaled into the lungs during general anaesthesia. Risk factors present in this case include emergency surgery and recent food intake. The rapid onset of respiratory symptoms, bilateral infiltrates, and hypoxia within hours of surgery are characteristic features.

Aspiration pneumonitis typically presents within 2-5 hours of the aspiration event with acute onset of dyspnoea, cough, infiltrates on chest imaging, and hypoxia. The presence of frothy sputum and bilateral crackles supports this diagnosis. The timing and circumstances make this the most likely diagnosis.

Pulmonary embolism would be less likely to cause bilateral infiltrates on chest X-ray. While surgery and COPD are risk factors for PE, the bilateral nature of the findings and the presence of productive cough make this diagnosis less likely. It is also very early in the post-operative recovery to have developed a PE.

Cardiogenic pulmonary oedema could be considered given his cardiac history, but the acute onset immediately post-surgery and fever would not be typical features. The timing strongly suggests an acute event related to the procedure.

COPD exacerbation typically presents with increased wheeze, cough and sputum production. While this patient has COPD, the sudden onset post-operatively, bilateral infiltrates, and frothy sputum are more suggestive of aspiration.

Acute respiratory distress syndrome can develop as a consequence of severe aspiration pneumonitis, but would typically evolve over 24-48 hours rather than presenting immediately post-operatively. However, this patient may progress to ARDS if their condition worsens.

Management of aspiration pneumonitis includes supplemental oxygen, positioning the patient upright, and close monitoring. Antibiotics are not routinely required unless bacterial infection is suspected. Some patients may require mechanical ventilation if respiratory failure develops.
learningPoints: Aspiration pneumonitis typically presents within hours of aspiration with lung infiltrates and hypoxia.
Risk factors for aspiration include emergency surgery, full stomach, and impaired consciousness.
Early recognition of aspiration pneumonitis is crucial as it can rapidly progress to ARDS.
references: Sanivarapu RR, Vaqar S, Gibson J. Aspiration Pneumonia. [Updated 2024 Mar 20]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK470459/
O'Connor S. Aspiration pneumonia and pneumonitis. Aust Prescr 2003;26:14-7. https://doi.org/10.18773/austprescr.2003.009

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
